behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at module handoff (HTTP handler -> signature verifier -> queue writer). You need the handler wired with raw-body extraction and queue boundary to catch real regressions; a pure unit of `verifyHmac()` can miss forwarding bugs. Rejected higher levels: behavior text only requires `401` + `not forwarded`, with no real network/OS/subprocess and no AI output dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run the webhook route in-process with real middleware stack and a fake/in-memory queue adapter. Send a request with mismatched `X-Signature-256` vs raw body; assert HTTP 401 and zero enqueue calls/writes.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is ordering across modules (handler + persistent queue + response path). Needs real queue write path (e.g., local DB/filesystem-backed queue), but not real external network or subprocess. Rejected System: behavior text is about write-before-ack ordering, not real HTTP/network failure modes.
behaviors.test_description: Start app in test mode with real persistent queue backend (local DB/file). Instrument timestamps or hooks: send valid webhook and assert queue write commit occurs before response completion (`200`).

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Bug appears in async orchestration between handler and downstream processor. In-process wiring with a blocked downstream worker is sufficient; no real OS/network primitive is required by the behavior text. Rejected System: no requirement for real subprocess/external HTTP, only non-blocking response behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + downstream consumer in-process. Make downstream processing deliberately slow (e.g., unresolved promise for N seconds). Send valid webhook and assert response returns quickly while processing is still in progress.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time/concurrency SLO (`within 3 seconds wall-clock`) under concurrent load; event-loop scheduling, socket backlog, and runtime behavior can hide in fakes. REAL-INFRA applies due explicit wall-clock and concurrent request behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run real Node HTTP server process and send concurrent real HTTP requests while earlier events trigger slow downstream work. Measure end-to-end wall-clock latency; assert responses stay under 3 seconds.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is typically at persistence boundary (unique key/idempotency check) across handler and queue store. Needs real module wiring and real store semantics (local DB/filesystem queue), not just a unit mock. Rejected System: behavior text is about duplicate-write prevention, not OS/network/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Use handler with real persistent queue store and idempotency key logic. Send same event ID twice (including near-concurrent submissions) and assert only one queue record exists.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This verifies error propagation/mapping from queue module to HTTP response contract (`503` + `Retry-After`). In-process queue implementation or controlled adapter state is enough; no real OS/network primitive required. Rejected System: behavior text depends on queue full signal handling, not external infra timing/network conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Run route with queue module configured to return a deterministic `queue full` error. Send valid webhook and assert HTTP 503 and presence/value format of `Retry-After` header.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This targets the real queue implementation and its persistence semantics; mocks would hide enforcement bugs. It needs multi-module integration with actual local storage backend, but not necessarily real external network/subprocess. Rejected System: behavior text focuses on capacity enforcement logic in queue implementation, not OS/network-specific behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate real persistent queue backend with small configured capacity. Enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one more and assert rejection/error plus unchanged stored count.
